title: Esther 9
subtitle: The Jews Defend Themselves; Purim Established

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Esther Saves Her People

sections.header: Verses 1–19 — The 13th of Adar: "The Reverse Occurred"

heb.paragraph: "The reverse occurred" (wənahapōk̅ hûʾ) — the verb nahapōk̅ is the book’s theological keyword. What was planned against the Jews happened to their enemies instead. The reversal is not partial but total. This single word is the book’s thesis.

heb.gloss: they did not lay hands on / they did not plunder
heb.paragraph: Three times the text notes the Jews "did not lay their hands on the plunder" (vv.10, 15, 16). Though the counter-decree authorised plundering, the Jews refused. This deliberate restraint echoes Saul’s failure with Agag — what Saul should have done (refuse the plunder, 1 Sam 15:19), the Jews now do.

hist.historical: On the 13th of Adar, the Jews defended themselves throughout the empire. In Susa alone, 500 men were killed including Haman’s ten sons. Esther requests an additional day in Susa (the 14th), and Haman’s sons are publicly displayed. The provincial Jews celebrate on the 14th; Susa Jews on the 15th — hence the two-day Purim observance.
hist.context: The narrator emphasises restraint over violence. The Jews kill their attackers but refuse the plunder. In a book that replays the Saul-Agag conflict, this detail is crucial: where Saul took Agag’s plunder and lost the kingdom, Mordecai’s community refuses it and preserves the people. The correction of Saul’s failure is complete.

refs.note: Saul took the plunder from Amalek and lost the kingdom. The Jews now refuse it.
Israel’s deliverance at the Sea — the Exodus model for the Purim victory.

notes.note: "The reverse occurred." Three words that summarise the entire book. The day intended for Jewish destruction becomes the day of their enemies’ defeat.
The Jews strike down their enemies in Susa. The text records numbers but not celebration of killing. The violence is described matter-of-factly, not triumphally.
"But they did not lay their hands on the plunder." First refusal. The restraint corrects Saul’s error with Agag — a deliberate echo.
Esther requests an additional day in Susa and the public display of Haman’s ten sons. The additional day is Esther’s own initiative — she acts with authority now, not just survival.
Calvin: the reversal is God’s signature. What the wicked intended is turned upon themselves. This is not coincidence but judgment.
Calvin: they refused the plunder though it was lawful. Restraint in victory is a higher virtue than victory itself.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes nahapōk̅ (niphal of hpk) means "was turned upside down" — a complete inversion of the expected outcome.
The NET connects the plunder refusal to Abraham’s refusal of Sodom’s goods (Gen 14:22–24) — the righteous do not profit from the destruction of the wicked.

Levenson: the Purim victory is the Exodus retold. As Israel defeated Egypt, the Jews defeat their Persian-era enemies. As Israel plundered Egypt (Exod 12:36), the Jews refuse plunder — improving on the pattern.
Levenson notes the plunder refusal corrects 1 Sam 15: what Saul (son of Kish) failed to do, Mordecai (also of Kish’s line) accomplishes.
Jobes: nahapōk̅ is the theological centre. The whole book exists to say this one word: reversed. Everything Haman planned happened to him instead.
Jobes on the three-fold plunder refusal: this is the narrator’s moral commentary. The Jews exercise power with restraint — they take only what justice requires.

heb.paragraph: The festival is named for the lot (pûr) Haman cast to choose the date of destruction (3:7). The name commemorates not the victory but the mechanism of the threat — the random chance that God governed. Purim celebrates the God who controls what pagans call fate.

"These days should be remembered (nizkārîm) and observed in every generation." Memory is the purpose of the festival. Purim exists so that no generation forgets: the reversal happened, and the hidden God was its author.

refs.note: Passover as memorial — the pattern Purim follows: a festival of deliverance that every generation must observe.
Festival observance as communal memory — joy rooted in historical rescue.

notes.note: Mordecai establishes Purim: annual observance on the 14th and 15th of Adar. Joy, feasting, giving to the poor. The festival combines celebration with charity.
The narrative summary: Haman plotted, cast the lot (pur), planned destruction. Esther interceded. Haman was hanged. The summary compresses ten chapters into four verses.
The obligation is binding on Jews and converts "without fail, in every generation." The festival is permanent. Purim is still celebrated today — the longest-running festival in the book.
Esther writes with "full authority" to confirm Purim. The orphan girl who concealed her identity now issues royal decrees under her own name. The arc is complete.
Calvin: the annual observance guards against forgetfulness. Every generation must hear again what God did, lest they take deliverance for granted.
Calvin: Esther’s confirming letter shows that the queen has grown from reluctant instrument to authoritative leader. God completes the work he begins.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes the Akkadian loanword pûr gives the festival its foreign-sounding name — a reminder that the story unfolds in exile, not in the homeland.
The NET observes "the command of Esther confirmed these regulations" gives the queen independent legislative authority — remarkable for the ancient world.
Levenson traces Purim’s development: from historical event to annual commemoration to canonical text. The book of Esther is itself a Purim text — read aloud every year at the festival.
Levenson: "never cease from among the Jews" — the festival’s permanence is the book’s final theological claim. What God reversed, God’s people remember forever.
Jobes: the festival is named after the lot, not the victory. Purim celebrates not Jewish power but divine sovereignty over what looks like chance. The name encodes the theology.
Jobes on Esther’s authority: she confirms Purim with "full authority" — her own name, her own seal. The concealed girl of chapter 2 is now the authoritative queen of chapter 9.

hist.context: The establishment of Purim is described twice: Mordecai’s letter (vv.20–28) and Esther’s confirmation (vv.29–32). The double authorisation — from the male leader and the female leader — reflects the book’s dual leadership. Both are needed; both carry authority. Esther’s letter has "full authority" (v.29).

ppl.name: Esther
ppl.role: Queen; confirms Purim with full authority
ppl.text: Issues the confirming letter under her own name. The concealed orphan is now the sovereign voice of Jewish identity in the Persian empire.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVOn the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, the month of Adar, the edict commanded by the king was to be carried out. On this day the enemies of the Jews had hoped to overpower them, but now the tables were turned and the Jews got the upper hand over those who hated them.ESVNow in the twelfth month, which is the month of Adar, on the thirteenth day of the same, when the king’s command and edict were about to be carried out, on the very day when the enemies of the Jews hoped to gain the mastery over them, the reverse occurred: the Jews gained mastery over those who hated them.NIVMordecai was prominent in the palace; his reputation spread throughout the provinces, and he became more and more powerful.ESVFor Mordecai was great in the king’s house, and his fame spread throughout all the provinces, for the man Mordecai grew more and more powerful.

rec.text: ""The reverse occurred." Three words that summarise the entire book. The day intended for Jewish destruction becomes the day of their enemies’ defeat."

rows.label: Purim Established — 9:20–28
rows.text: Named for the lot. Celebrated forever. Memory as obligation.

rows.label: Esther Confirms — 9:29–32
rows.text: Full authority. The concealed girl issues decrees.

themes.note: Sovereignty at 9: the lot’s name on the festival declares God’s control over fate. Justice: the reversal is exact. Worship rises: Purim is liturgical celebration.
